1 # my-harness 2 3 Personal harness for agentic workflows: reusable agents and GitHub Actions 4 glue that I drop into projects. 5 6 ## Contents 7 8 - [What's here](#whats-here) 9 - [Use this harness in another project](#use-this-harness-in-another-project) 10 - [GitHub Actions configuration](#github-actions-configuration) 11 - [Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger)](#jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger) 12 - [Adding a new agent](#adding-a-new-agent) 13 14 ## What's here 15 16 - [`agents/pr-review`](agents/pr-review) — comment-only PR reviewer. 17 - [`agents/jira-ticket`](agents/jira-ticket) — daily Jira backlog groomer. 18 - [`agents/junior-dev`](agents/junior-dev) — headless implementer for small 19 tickets (SP ≤ 3). 20 - [`agents/senior-dev`](agents/senior-dev) — OpenSpec-driven implementer 21 for complex tickets, with a human-in-the-loop pause on the spec PR. 22 - [`agents/agent-improver`](agents/agent-improver) — meta-agent that 23 proposes prompt edits from execution logs. 24 - [`.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml`](.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml) 25 — deterministic Jira → junior/senior router on the **Dev Ready** 26 transition. 27 - [`scripts/bootstrap.sh`](scripts/bootstrap.sh) — copy this harness into 28 another repo (see below). 29 - [`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](docs/agentic-workflow.md) — how the pieces 30 fit together. 31 32 --- 33 34 ## Use this harness in another project 35 36 `scripts/bootstrap.sh` `rsync`s every tracked file from this repo into a 37 target repo, skipping per-clone state (`.env`, `.jira/config.yml`, 38 `bin/.jira-bin`, agent-log history). Default is dry-run. 39 40 ```sh 41 # from anywhere 42 bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/bootstrap.sh ~/Dev/other-repo # dry-run 43 bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/bootstrap.sh ~/Dev/other-repo --apply # do it 44 ``` 45 46 What gets copied: `.github/workflows/`, `agents/`, `.claude/`, `.mcp.json`, 47 `scripts/`, `tools/`, `docs/`, `openspec/`, `.env.example`, `.jira/.gitkeep`. 48 49 What's left alone in the target: 50 - The target's existing `README.md` — never overwritten. 51 - The target's existing `.gitignore` — harness rules are appended in a 52 delimited `# --- my-harness ---` block. Re-running won't duplicate. 53 - Anything not tracked in the harness (project source, `node_modules`, etc.). 54 55 After bootstrapping, run the per-clone setup in the target repo: 56 57 ```sh 58 # 1. project-local jira binary into ./bin/ (no brew needed) 59 bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh 60 61 # 2. paste tokens into a gitignored .env 62 cp .env.example .env 63 # JIRA_API_TOKEN=... https://id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens 64 # CONTEXT7_API_KEY=... https://context7.com/dashboard 65 66 # 3. wire this clone to a Jira instance / project 67 bash scripts/jira-init.sh 68 ``` 69 70 `jira-init.sh` writes `.jira/config.yml` (gitignored). For day-to-day CLI 71 use: `source .env && export JIRA_CONFIG_FILE=$PWD/.jira/config.yml`, or 72 let the `bin/jira` wrapper handle it (it auto-injects `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` 73 from the repo root). 74 75 --- 76 77 ## GitHub Actions configuration 78 79 The workflows read repo **secrets** (sensitive) and repo **variables** 80 (non-sensitive) from *Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions*. Set 81 these once per target repo. 82 83 ### Secrets 84 85 | Name | Used by | Source | 86 |---|---|---| 87 | `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` | every Claude-driven workflow | Run `claude` locally and grab the token, or use an Anthropic API key | 88 | `JIRA_API_TOKEN` | jira-ticket, orchestrator, junior-dev, senior-dev | | 89 | `CONTEXT7_API_KEY` | workflows that pass it through to the Context7 MCP | | 90 | `SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN` | jira-ticket Slack summary | Slack app *Bot User OAuth Token* (`xoxb-…`) with `chat:write` | 91 | `DEV_PAT` *(optional)* | senior-dev / junior-dev when they need to push to a protected branch | Fine-grained PAT, `Contents: read/write` + `Pull requests: read/write`. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` if unset | 92 93 ### Variables 94 95 | Name | Required? | Purpose | 96 |---|---|---| 97 | `JIRA_SERVER` | yes | e.g. `https://yourworkspace.atlassian.net` | 98 | `JIRA_LOGIN` | yes | Atlassian account email | 99 | `JIRA_PROJECT_KEY` | yes | Project the agents groom / route on | 100 | `JIRA_BOARD_ID` | optional | Defaults to `0` | 101 | `SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID` | only if you want jira-ticket Slack summaries | Channel ID (not name) — `C0123456789` | 102 103 ### Things to review per repo 104 105 Every workflow under `.github/workflows/` was written for *this* repo. 106 After bootstrapping, scan the new repo's copy for: 107 108 - **Cron times** in `jira-ticket.yml` (currently `17 7 * * *` UTC). 109 - **Triggers** (`pull_request` types, `pull_request_review` types) in 110 `pr-review.yml` and `senior-dev-resume.yml` — adjust if the project's 111 branching model differs. 112 - **`max_turns` / `timeout-minutes`** on dev-agent workflows — defaults 113 bias toward small tickets. 114 115 --- 116 117 ## Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger) 118 119 The orchestrator only runs when Jira tells it a ticket is ready. Wire 120 this **once per Jira project** (the rule lives in Jira automation, not 121 in code). 122 123 ### 1. Create a fine-grained GitHub PAT 124 125 In *github.com → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → 126 Fine-grained tokens*: 127 128 - **Repository access:** only the target repo. 129 - **Permissions:** 130 - `Contents: Read` 131 - `Actions: Read and write` 132 133 Copy the token — you'll paste it into Jira next. 134 135 ### 2. Store the PAT as a Jira automation secret 136 137 In Jira: *Project settings → Automation → … → Manage secrets*. 138 Add a secret, e.g. `GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT`, paste the token, save. 139 Jira automation rules can reference it as `{{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`. 140 141 ### 3. Add the automation rule 142 143 *Project settings → Automation → Create rule*: 144 145 - **Trigger:** *Issue transitioned* → To status: **Dev Ready**. 146 - **(Optional) Conditions:** issue type is *Story* / *Sub-task*; project 147 is the right one; story points field is set (the orchestrator fails 148 loudly on unpointed tickets, so you can pre-filter here). 149 - **Action:** *Send web request*. 150 151 | Field | Value | 152 |---|---| 153 | URL | `https://api.github.com/repos///dispatches` | 154 | Method | `POST` | 155 | Headers | `Authorization: Bearer {{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`
`Accept: application/vnd.github+json`
`Content-Type: application/json` | 156 | Body type | Custom data | 157 | Body | `{"event_type":"jira-dev-ready","client_payload":{"ticket_key":"{{issue.key}}"}}` | 158 159 Tick *Wait for response* if you want failures to surface in the rule's 160 audit log. 161 162 Save and enable the rule. 163 164 ### 4. Verify 165 166 Move a pointed ticket to **Dev Ready**. Within ~30 seconds: 167 168 - *Jira → Project settings → Automation → Audit log* shows the rule 169 firing with HTTP 204 (the GitHub dispatches endpoint returns 204 on 170 success, no body). 171 - *GitHub → Actions → Orchestrator* shows a new run with 172 `repository_dispatch` as the trigger. 173 - The orchestrator either dispatches `junior-dev.yml` / `senior-dev.yml` 174 by story points, or fails the run with a "no story points" error. 175 176 ### 5. Linking PRs back to tickets 177 178 Jira's **Smart Commits** auto-link if the Jira key (e.g. `KAN-42`) 179 appears in: 180 181 - Commit messages 182 - Branch names 183 - PR titles or descriptions 184 185 The dev agents already prefix branches and PR titles with the ticket 186 key, so links appear under *Development* on the ticket once the GitHub 187 ↔ Jira app is connected (*Apps → GitHub for Jira* on the Atlassian 188 side, one-time per workspace). 189 190 --- 191 192 ## Adding a new agent 193 194 1. Drop the prompt at `agents//prompt.md`. 195 2. Wire a workflow at `.github/workflows/.yml` that feeds the 196 prompt to `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` (or `claude -p` for 197 implementers). 198 3. Make sure the secrets/vars it references are listed above; add any 199 new ones to this README. 200 201 202 203 204

file.content:
# my-harness Personal harness for agentic workflows: reusable agents and GitHub Actions glue that I drop into projects. ## Contents - [What's here](#whats-here) - [Use this harness in another project](#use-this-harness-in-another-project) - [GitHub Actions configuration](#github-actions-configuration) - [Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger)](#jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger) - [Adding a new agent](#adding-a-new-agent) ## What's here - [`agents/pr-review`](agents/pr-review) — comment-only PR reviewer. - [`agents/jira-ticket`](agents/jira-ticket) — daily Jira backlog groomer. - [`agents/junior-dev`](agents/junior-dev) — headless implementer for small tickets (SP ≤ 3). - [`agents/senior-dev`](agents/senior-dev) — OpenSpec-driven implementer for complex tickets, with a human-in-the-loop pause on the spec PR. - [`agents/agent-improver`](agents/agent-improver) — meta-agent that proposes prompt edits from execution logs. - [`.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml`](.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml) — deterministic Jira → junior/senior router on the **Dev Ready** transition. - [`scripts/bootstrap.sh`](scripts/bootstrap.sh) — copy this harness into another repo (see below). - [`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](docs/agentic-workflow.md) — how the pieces fit together. --- ## Use this harness in another project `scripts/bootstrap.sh` `rsync`s every tracked file from this repo into a target repo, skipping per-clone state (`.env`, `.jira/config.yml`, `bin/.jira-bin`, agent-log history). Default is dry-run. ```sh # from anywhere bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/bootstrap.sh ~/Dev/other-repo # dry-run bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/bootstrap.sh ~/Dev/other-repo --apply # do it ``` What gets copied: `.github/workflows/`, `agents/`, `.claude/`, `.mcp.json`, `scripts/`, `tools/`, `docs/`, `openspec/`, `.env.example`, `.jira/.gitkeep`. What's left alone in the target: - The target's existing `README.md` — never overwritten. - The target's existing `.gitignore` — harness rules are appended in a delimited `# --- my-harness ---` block. Re-running won't duplicate. - Anything not tracked in the harness (project source, `node_modules`, etc.). After bootstrapping, run the per-clone setup in the target repo: ```sh # 1. project-local jira binary into ./bin/ (no brew needed) bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh # 2. paste tokens into a gitignored .env cp .env.example .env # JIRA_API_TOKEN=... https://id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens # CONTEXT7_API_KEY=... https://context7.com/dashboard # 3. wire this clone to a Jira instance / project bash scripts/jira-init.sh ``` `jira-init.sh` writes `.jira/config.yml` (gitignored). For day-to-day CLI use: `source .env && export JIRA_CONFIG_FILE=$PWD/.jira/config.yml`, or let the `bin/jira` wrapper handle it (it auto-injects `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` from the repo root). --- ## GitHub Actions configuration The workflows read repo **secrets** (sensitive) and repo **variables** (non-sensitive) from *Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions*. Set these once per target repo. ### Secrets | Name | Used by | Source | |---|---|---| | `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` | every Claude-driven workflow | Run `claude` locally and grab the token, or use an Anthropic API key | | `JIRA_API_TOKEN` | jira-ticket, orchestrator, junior-dev, senior-dev | | | `CONTEXT7_API_KEY` | workflows that pass it through to the Context7 MCP | | | `SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN` | jira-ticket Slack summary | Slack app *Bot User OAuth Token* (`xoxb-…`) with `chat:write` | | `DEV_PAT` *(optional)* | senior-dev / junior-dev when they need to push to a protected branch | Fine-grained PAT, `Contents: read/write` + `Pull requests: read/write`. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` if unset | ### Variables | Name | Required? | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `JIRA_SERVER` | yes | e.g. `https://yourworkspace.atlassian.net` | | `JIRA_LOGIN` | yes | Atlassian account email | | `JIRA_PROJECT_KEY` | yes | Project the agents groom / route on | | `JIRA_BOARD_ID` | optional | Defaults to `0` | | `SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID` | only if you want jira-ticket Slack summaries | Channel ID (not name) — `C0123456789` | ### Things to review per repo Every workflow under `.github/workflows/` was written for *this* repo. After bootstrapping, scan the new repo's copy for: - **Cron times** in `jira-ticket.yml` (currently `17 7 * * *` UTC). - **Triggers** (`pull_request` types, `pull_request_review` types) in `pr-review.yml` and `senior-dev-resume.yml` — adjust if the project's branching model differs. - **`max_turns` / `timeout-minutes`** on dev-agent workflows — defaults bias toward small tickets. --- ## Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger) The orchestrator only runs when Jira tells it a ticket is ready. Wire this **once per Jira project** (the rule lives in Jira automation, not in code). ### 1. Create a fine-grained GitHub PAT In *github.com → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens*: - **Repository access:** only the target repo. - **Permissions:** - `Contents: Read` - `Actions: Read and write` Copy the token — you'll paste it into Jira next. ### 2. Store the PAT as a Jira automation secret In Jira: *Project settings → Automation → … → Manage secrets*. Add a secret, e.g. `GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT`, paste the token, save. Jira automation rules can reference it as `{{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`. ### 3. Add the automation rule *Project settings → Automation → Create rule*: - **Trigger:** *Issue transitioned* → To status: **Dev Ready**. - **(Optional) Conditions:** issue type is *Story* / *Sub-task*; project is the right one; story points field is set (the orchestrator fails loudly on unpointed tickets, so you can pre-filter here). - **Action:** *Send web request*. | Field | Value | |---|---| | URL | `https://api.github.com/repos///dispatches` | | Method | `POST` | | Headers | `Authorization: Bearer {{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`
`Accept: application/vnd.github+json`
`Content-Type: application/json` | | Body type | Custom data | | Body | `{"event_type":"jira-dev-ready","client_payload":{"ticket_key":"{{issue.key}}"}}` | Tick *Wait for response* if you want failures to surface in the rule's audit log. Save and enable the rule. ### 4. Verify Move a pointed ticket to **Dev Ready**. Within ~30 seconds: - *Jira → Project settings → Automation → Audit log* shows the rule firing with HTTP 204 (the GitHub dispatches endpoint returns 204 on success, no body). - *GitHub → Actions → Orchestrator* shows a new run with `repository_dispatch` as the trigger. - The orchestrator either dispatches `junior-dev.yml` / `senior-dev.yml` by story points, or fails the run with a "no story points" error. ### 5. Linking PRs back to tickets Jira's **Smart Commits** auto-link if the Jira key (e.g. `KAN-42`) appears in: - Commit messages - Branch names - PR titles or descriptions The dev agents already prefix branches and PR titles with the ticket key, so links appear under *Development* on the ticket once the GitHub ↔ Jira app is connected (*Apps → GitHub for Jira* on the Atlassian side, one-time per workspace). --- ## Adding a new agent 1. Drop the prompt at `agents//prompt.md`. 2. Wire a workflow at `.github/workflows/.yml` that feeds the prompt to `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` (or `claude -p` for implementers). 3. Make sure the secrets/vars it references are listed above; add any new ones to this README.

content.thinking:
Let me verify the anchor links in the table of contents. GitHub Markdown converts heading text to anchors by:
1. Converting to lowercase
2. Removing anything that's not a word character, hyphen, or space
3. Converting spaces to hyphens

Let me check each link:

1. `[What's here](#whats-here)` → "What's here" → lowercase: "what's here" → remove apostrophe: "whats here" → spaces to hyphens: "whats-here" ✓

2. `[Use this harness in another project](#use-this-harness-in-another-project)` → "Use this harness in another project" → lowercase + spaces to hyphens: "use-this-harness-in-another-project" ✓
3. `[GitHub Actions configuration](#github-actions-configuration)` → "GitHub Actions configuration" → lowercase + spaces to hyphens: "github-actions-configuration" ✓

4. `[Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger)](#jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger)` → "Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger)" → lowercase: "jira → github automation (dev ready trigger)" → remove special chars (→, parentheses) and convert spaces to hyphens: "jira---github-automation-dev-ready-trigger"... Actually, I need to think through this more carefully. The arrow and parentheses get stripped, but I'm not sure exactly how GitHub handles multiple consecutive hyphens.

Let me trace through the algorithm: take the heading text, convert to lowercase, remove anything that's not a word character/space/hyphen, then replace spaces with hyphens. So "jira → github automation (dev ready trigger)" becomes "jira  github automation dev ready trigger" after stripping the arrow and parens, which gives us "jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger" with that double hyphen between jira and github.

I'm verifying all the anchor links in the table of contents match their corresponding headings, and they all check out correctly.

input.command: gh pr comment 59 --body "Looks good to me. The five anchor links are all correct (including the double-hyphen in \`#jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger\` for the arrow character in that heading), and the change is scoped purely to documentation."
